As a plantation owner in 1853, the safety and security of my plantation were of utmost importance. If someone tried to run away, I would take immediate and decisive action to prevent it. I would notify my overseers and any trusted workers to keep a close watch on the individuals who had attempted to escape. If they were still on my property, I would gather my workers and attempt to capture them quickly, ensuring that they pose no immediate threat to the others on my plantation.

Once I had the fugitives in custody, I would assess the situation and determine the severity of their attempt to escape. If they were merely trying to leave their assigned labor and had not caused significant disruption, I might choose to give them a stern warning and a short confinement, hoping to deter them and others from attempting similar actions in the future.

However, if the attempted escape posed a serious threat to the safety and security of my plantation, or if the individuals involved demonstrated a clear intention of causing havoc, I would exercise even more drastic measures. I might confine them in a secure area, such as a small building under guard, and report the incident to local authorities to ensure that they are dealt with according to the laws and regulations at that time.
